source_files: pattern_analysis.json (12 belief patterns BL-01 through BL-12)

surface_beliefs.belief: My putting inconsistency is the biggest thing holding my scores back

supporting_quotes.quote: Saturday I shot 82. I hit the ball pretty well... I hit 10 greens in Regulation and yet ended up shooting 82. I could not buy a putt.
people stop and come over to me at the range and are amazed how well I hit the ball, they will say 'god you must be off scratch.' I say 'no im off 11 I can't putt'
My putting has sucked forever; I am a 5 but when I am playing well, I am striking the ball like a scratch and putting like a 12.
My putting has disappeared. My swing is still ok. My drives go miles. But my scores have taken a massive hit and can't seem to score!!
I'm a 6 right now and I swear I'd be a 2 almost overnight if I just got to be a good (not great) putter.

surface_beliefs.marketing_impact: RS1 messaging should lead with the identity of the 'good ball-striker who loses strokes on the green.' This is the largest self-identified audience. They already believe putting is the problem -- RS1 just needs to name the specific cause (face drift) and offer the specific solution (forward weighting).

surface_beliefs.belief: Confidence is the most important factor in putting

supporting_quotes.quote: Being fitted for it gives so much confidence and cant see past it at the moment.
So I'm left asking myself, 'How much is confidence worth?' Because confidence was the primary thing this putter gave me over my Atlanta.
I believe that confidence has been my missing link to better putting and that's what the DF3 gives me.
First, yes - I need to be more confident. I'm a wreck on the greens right now - total neuroses. Shaking over every putt.
The key for me is being able to read greens well. This gives me the confidence to get the ball to the hole.

surface_beliefs.marketing_impact: Every RS1 engineering claim must ultimately resolve to a confidence payoff. Features do not sell putters -- the feeling of standing over a putt and knowing it will go where you aim it sells putters. RS1 marketing arc: technology -> face stability -> consistent start lines -> confidence.

surface_beliefs.belief: Face angle at impact determines where the putt goes

supporting_quotes.quote: Your face angle at impact was more than 1 degree open or closed. According to Quintic - face angle at impact needs to be less than 1 degree open or closed if not putt will miss.
In my opinion, the reason why people miss short putts is because of inconsistent rotational control of the putter head during the stroke.
the more excessive the rotation you have the harder it is to return the putter to impact consistently.
I have two Scotty Cameron Newport putters and they will pull or push on every putt. This is dead straight.

surface_beliefs.marketing_impact: This is the scientific foundation for RS1. Golfers who already accept this belief are pre-qualified for face-stability technology. RS1 should reinforce this belief with data (Quintic measurements, face angle tolerances) and then position Heavy Forward Weighting as the engineering solution.

surface_beliefs.belief: The right putter can dramatically reduce strokes

supporting_quotes.quote: I used my df3 for the first time yesterday and I putted like a pro. No lessons and this thing took at least 6-7 strokes off my score.
5 rounds later, the putter has probably dropped 10 strokes for me.
According to Arccos, with others I was a -1.2 vs my prefered handicap putting... DF3 - I am actually a +1.1 in putting... So a total gain of 2.3
This putter took 8 strokes off my game. All my golf buddies are buying one.
Distance control from 30 feet before was a spread of 6 feet... Now, with the same drill, I am within 2 feet. The change was immediate; night and day.

surface_beliefs.marketing_impact: Stroke-count specificity is the market's own measurement language. RS1 testimonials and claims should always include specific numbers: 'saved 3-5 strokes per round,' 'went from 35 to 31 putts per round.' Abstract quality claims are weak; countable improvement is persuasive.

surface_beliefs.belief: You should try before you buy a premium putter

supporting_quotes.quote: Given how much these putters cost, I didn't want to make a purchase decision based only on how it performed in the studio.
Everytime i demo them in store, i make everything. But i can also say the same with the other putters. Its so easy to putt in store. Its a different story on the course.
Like sports teams, putters can look great on paper but perform poorly on grass! Testing inside isn't a true reflection.
This is the kind of real analysis that I love to see. I don't really care what an influencer 'says'. Show me the data.

surface_beliefs.marketing_impact: RS1 launch strategy should include a risk-reduction mechanism -- guarantee, trial period, or demo availability. The try-before-buy belief is strongest among the exact buyer RS1 targets (informed, research-driven, $400+ budget). Without a trial path, this belief becomes a conversion blocker.

surface_beliefs.belief: Practice is what makes you a good putter, not equipment

supporting_quotes.quote: You will never be a good putter if you think it is all about the putter. Putting is about practicing to send the ball consistently in a straight line.
Nothing will improve your putting more than hours of practice. There is no need to keep changing putters, they aren't magic wands.
i've used every variation of putters and grips and techniques but the only thing that helps me is time on the practice putting green.
I've been trying to buy a game for almost 40 years. The only thing that's lowered my handicap all these years is actually playing golf.

surface_beliefs.marketing_impact: RS1 must not attack this belief -- it is deeply respected in golf culture. Instead, reframe: 'Practice matters. The RS1 makes your practice more effective by eliminating the one variable you cannot practice away -- face drift caused by rear CG.' Position technology as amplifying skill, not replacing it.

surface_beliefs.belief: A putter fitting is essential for a premium putter to work

supporting_quotes.quote: The key is to get the correct lie angle, otherwise you're throwing your money away
Little concerned that you were not 'fitted' for lie angle and length first - thought that was the whole reason for this make of putter!!
After learning more about fitting, I realized that putter fitting looks very simple and straightforward but is actually very very very very complicated and should be left to the experts.
Its the most used club in you bag per hole. Get a fitting from a pro, not some youtube joe.

surface_beliefs.marketing_impact: RS1 must address the fitting question directly. If the 74-degree lie angle is a 'one size fits most' approach, the marketing must explain why. If fitting is recommended, RS1 needs a fitting pathway that is not dependent on big-box stores (which are distrusted).

surface_beliefs.belief: Zero-torque / face-stable putters work for some people but not everyone

supporting_quotes.quote: No putter will work for everyone. The 'zero' torque putter is a good option for those that stability will help. Some need more face rotation.
If by 'work' you mean being comfortable with it then you are absolutely correct. Some people will just never be comfortable enough to use it and trust it.
it all depends on how you stroke the putter. Some players have a strong arc, some have a slight arc, and some swing it straight back and through like a pendulum.
Personally, i found it helped me reduce one of the variables i had with putting, which was face squaring at impact during putting, that's pretty much it

surface_beliefs.marketing_impact: RS1 should position itself as working with ALL stroke types by emphasizing that forward weighting reduces face drift regardless of arc. Unlike LAB's zero-torque (which some feel 'fights' their natural arc), RS1's forward CG works with natural stroke mechanics. This is a key differentiator.

surface_beliefs.belief: Premium putters should look good at address

supporting_quotes.quote: Love my Lab 2.1 but it is strange looking
a lot of people (myself included) are feel and visual players. Especially with putters, I have to like what I see when I look down at the putter
I had a oz1i, I went back to the TM Spider I'm visually more comfortable over the ball with spider
Thanks to L.A.B. Golf, the golfing consumer is more tolerant of odd-looking but high-performing putters than ever.

surface_beliefs.marketing_impact: RS1's mallet design must be visually appealing at address. If it looks like a conventional premium mallet (not a 'branding iron'), this is a massive competitive advantage over LAB. Marketing should feature at-address photography prominently.

surface_beliefs.belief: How a putter feels and sounds at impact matters as much as performance

supporting_quotes.quote: Not going 2 spend $600 4 a putter that sounds like shit!
I have not felt a softer putter that made me more confident. I ordered it last week.
My two biggest issues with the LABs I've tried are that I struggle with the shaft lean and don't like the feel off the aluminum face.
The Mezz had more of a high-pitched 'ting' that I really didn't like.

surface_beliefs.marketing_impact: RS1 must deliver premium feel and sound. LAB's aluminum face is a consistent complaint. If RS1 uses a different face material or insert, this is a selling point. Feel-based language ('buttery,' 'solid,' 'confident') must be prominent in copy alongside engineering claims.

surface_beliefs.belief: Tour player usage validates (or invalidates) putter technology

supporting_quotes.quote: Adam Scott first used a L.A.B. Golf Directed Force (DF) 2.1 putter on the PGA Tour in 2019
Notice the best putters in the world don't use lab, the guys that struggle use a lab
How many LAB putters have walked away with victory on the PGA tour this year? Zero?
Broomstick MEZZ MAX is by far the best in show. Adam Scott just placed 2nd in his best showing and he used the MEZZ MAX Broomstick.

surface_beliefs.marketing_impact: RS1 needs a tour validation strategy. Without tour usage, skeptics will dismiss it. Options: pro staff, mini-tour adoption, endorsement from a respected teaching professional. If tour validation is not available at launch, the marketing must address this head-on with engineering proof instead.

surface_beliefs.belief: The putter is the most used club in the bag and deserves the most investment

supporting_quotes.quote: players will spend 600-700 pounds on a driver which they will use probs 6-10 times a round so spending the same on a putter which will be used around 32ish times a round shouldn't be an issue
People eagerly pull the trigger on buying a $600 driver but never think about getting fit for a putter like this. IMO it's worth it!
Modern drivers now regularly cost over 500 pounds, yet seem to change every six months... We use our putter more often, will hopefully keep it longer.
Its the most used club in you bag per hole.

surface_beliefs.marketing_impact: The per-use cost rationalization is already established in the market. RS1 should lean into this math: 'You use your putter 30+ times per round. That is more than any other club. Why is it the last one you invest in?' At $399, the per-stroke cost argument is even stronger than for $500+ putters.

implicit_beliefs.belief: The next putter might be 'the one' that finally fixes my putting
evidence: Golfers average 5-15+ putters over their playing lifetime
The putter carousel (buy-try-discard) is the dominant behavioral pattern (EP-01, frequency 74)
Permanence language when they finally find one: 'never going back,' 'not going anywhere'
implicit_beliefs.behavioral_indicator: Continuous putter purchasing despite repeated disappointment; willingness to spend $300-600+ repeatedly on new putters; keeping multiple putters and rotating them
implicit_beliefs.marketing_impact: RS1 can position as 'the last stop on the putter carousel' -- but only if it explains WHY it is different from every putter they have already tried. The difference must be mechanical (forward CG), not aspirational (vague promises). Acknowledge the carousel: 'We know you have tried everything.'

implicit_beliefs.belief: Peer results matter more than brand marketing
evidence: YouTube/forum research before purchase is near-universal (BH-02, frequency 44)
Golfers specifically seek negative reviews to validate skepticism
Evangelism after conversion is the most powerful marketing channel (BH-07, frequency 42)
implicit_beliefs.behavioral_indicator: Hours spent on YouTube reviews and forum threads before purchase; asking playing partners to try their putter; sharing specific stroke-count improvements on forums
implicit_beliefs.marketing_impact: RS1 marketing should prioritize peer testimonial content over brand advertising. User-generated content, real stroke-count data, and forum presence will drive conversion more effectively than polished ads. Seed early units to forum-active golfers who will share results.

implicit_beliefs.belief: If it works in the store, it might not work on the course
evidence: Practice green vs course performance gap is near-universal (EP-02, frequency 45)
Golfers explicitly distrust in-store putter testing (Q0471, Q0266)
The adaptation period means first-round results are unreliable (EP-05, frequency 35)
implicit_beliefs.behavioral_indicator: Requesting to take putters home for extended testing; citing on-course stats rather than range performance; tracking Arccos or stroke data over multiple rounds
implicit_beliefs.marketing_impact: RS1 proof points must come from on-course performance, not practice-green demos. Marketing should feature 5-round, 10-round, and season-long data. Any guarantee or trial period should be measured in rounds, not days.

implicit_beliefs.belief: My putting problems are partly my fault, partly equipment
evidence: Self-blame vs equipment-blame oscillation is constant (EM-05, frequency 46)
Golfers swing between 'it's my stroke' and 'this putter doesn't work for me' within the same thread
The relief of finding a putter that works is described in medical-recovery language
implicit_beliefs.behavioral_indicator: Trying technique fixes (grips, stances, lessons) AND equipment changes simultaneously; attributing good rounds to 'finally figuring something out' and bad rounds to 'the putter'; keeping old putters as fallbacks
implicit_beliefs.marketing_impact: RS1 must bridge this oscillation: 'It IS partly you AND partly your equipment. Your stroke provides the motion. The RS1 ensures the face stays square through that motion. Both matter.' This both/and positioning respects the golfer's intelligence while validating equipment investment.

implicit_beliefs.belief: A putter that looks unconventional will invite ridicule
evidence: LAB's 'branding iron' mockery is documented and consistent (BL-10, frequency 25)
Golfers who own unconventional putters preemptively address aesthetics: 'I know it looks weird, but...'
Visual comfort at address correlates with on-course performance
implicit_beliefs.behavioral_indicator: Defending putter appearance unprompted; joking about aesthetics before playing partners comment; switching back to conventional putters despite better performance with unconventional ones
implicit_beliefs.marketing_impact: If RS1 looks like a premium mallet (not unconventional), this is a major selling point. Marketing should feature the putter at address from the golfer's perspective, showing conventional, premium aesthetics. 'Tour-caliber looks with breakthrough engineering underneath.'

implicit_beliefs.belief: More expensive does not always mean better
evidence: TEMU/counterfeit LAB clones reportedly perform 'identically' (Q1398)
Golfers finding old, cheap putters that outperform premium ones (EP-09, frequency 28)
Skepticism that premium pricing reflects engineering rather than brand/marketing (BL-03, frequency 38)
implicit_beliefs.behavioral_indicator: Buying used putters on secondhand markets; comparing cheap clones to originals; making the 'it's the Indian, not the arrow' argument while simultaneously shopping for new putters
implicit_beliefs.marketing_impact: RS1 must justify its $399 price through engineering transparency, not brand cachet. Show the CG location data, the face angle measurements, the manufacturing process. Make the engineering undeniable so the price feels earned. Differentiate from counterfeits by proving the engineering cannot be replicated cheaply.

implicit_beliefs.belief: Once I find the right putter, I will never need another
evidence: Permanence language from converts: 'never going back,' 'best putter I've ever owned,' 'it's not going anywhere' (LP-06, frequency 41)
Long-term loyalty lock-in: golfers who commit for 1+ years describe inability to return to traditional putters (EP-11, frequency 24)
The 'last putter' aspiration appears in both Hope and Competitor Mechanism buckets
implicit_beliefs.behavioral_indicator: Describing putter purchases in lifetime terms; comparing current putter to all previous ones; refusing to even try other putters after finding 'the one'
implicit_beliefs.marketing_impact: RS1 can own the 'last putter you will ever buy' positioning -- but must earn it with technology that creates genuine lock-in. If Heavy Forward Weighting creates the same 'can't go back' effect that LAB's zero-torque does (where traditional putters feel 'alive' in a bad way), this becomes RS1's strongest retention mechanism.

implicit_beliefs.belief: The putter industry has been selling us the same thing for decades
evidence: Scotty Cameron worship-to-disillusion arc (EP-07, frequency 34)
Belief that premium putters are marketing over engineering (BL-03, frequency 38)
Skepticism about 'zero torque' as a fad (Q0472, Q0473, Q0307)
implicit_beliefs.behavioral_indicator: Cycling through heritage brands then gravitating toward technology-differentiated brands; responding positively to engineering explanations over marketing language; seeking independent test data (MyGolfSpy) over brand claims
implicit_beliefs.marketing_impact: RS1 has an opportunity to position as the engineering insurgent against the marketing establishment. 'Most putters are the same technology in different shapes. RS1 is fundamentally different because the CG sits in front of the shaft, not behind it.' The engineering story must be simple, visual, and provable.

implicit_beliefs.belief: Big-box fitting experiences are unreliable sales pitches
evidence: Widespread distrust of Golf Galaxy and PGA Superstore fittings (BL-09, frequency 22)
Golfers describing being pushed toward inventory the store needed to move (Q1397)
Conflicting fitting results from different stores on same golfer (Q0368)
implicit_beliefs.behavioral_indicator: Driving hours for independent fitters; requesting specific technology (SAM PuttLab) rather than general fittings; posting about fitting experiences with skepticism
implicit_beliefs.marketing_impact: RS1's DTC (direct-to-consumer) channel is an advantage here. Position the Performance Golf fitting experience as independent, engineering-based, and golfer-first. If RS1 offers a virtual or at-home fitting tool, it directly addresses this implicit distrust.

implicit_beliefs.belief: Adapting to new putter technology requires patience most golfers do not have
evidence: Adaptation period described as 2-3 rounds minimum, sometimes months (EP-05, frequency 35)
Golfers who quit during adaptation blame the putter, not the learning curve (Q0215, Q1344)
Those who push through describe dramatic long-term improvement
implicit_beliefs.behavioral_indicator: Returning putters after 2-3 poor rounds; describing adaptation struggles in forums then either pushing through or reverting; comparing initial results to long-term results
implicit_beliefs.marketing_impact: RS1 must set adaptation expectations during onboarding. Include a '3-round commitment' message: 'The first round reveals the technology. The third round reveals the results.' If RS1's adaptation curve is shorter than LAB's (because forward weighting works WITH natural stroke rather than requiring a new one), this is a critical selling point.

limiting_beliefs.belief: It's my stroke, not the putter -- equipment can't fix bad technique
limiting_beliefs.how_it_limits: Prevents golfers from investing $399+ in a putter because they believe the money should go to lessons instead. Creates guilt about equipment purchases and a 'buying your way out of trouble' shame narrative.
limiting_beliefs.counter_belief_needed: Your stroke provides the motion. But your putter determines what happens to the face during that motion. The RS1 keeps the face stable through YOUR stroke, making YOUR technique more effective.

supporting_quotes.quote: If your stroke isn't consistently correct, it won't make a difference what putter you use.

I think that buying your way out of trouble is just a quick fix - you'll feel more confidence with the new stick initially but you know full well that the club hasn't changed over the last 10 weeks while your putting has. That means it can't be the stick, it's you.
The data all shows that tech in this particular tool doesn't matter much... What is left? Confidence, preference and the person using the tool.
I find it funny that golfers have that mentality that they can buy their way into better scores. Just practice more.

limiting_beliefs.belief: All premium putters are basically the same -- the differences are marketing
limiting_beliefs.how_it_limits: Commoditizes putter technology and makes the golfer price-sensitive rather than value-sensitive. If all putters are the same, why pay $399 when a $150 putter exists?
limiting_beliefs.counter_belief_needed: Most putters ARE the same -- they all have the CG behind the shaft, which causes face drift. The RS1 is structurally different because the CG sits in front of the shaft. This is not marketing. It is physics.

supporting_quotes.quote: I've noticed some putters are $100 while others are $400+ and even more from custom shops. For people who have tried both, did you actually notice a difference?
The data all shows that tech in this particular tool doesn't matter much
You don't need an expensive putter to putt well. Cleveland Hb soft are great putters and not overly expensive. A $1000 LAB isn't a magic wand.
I went on to TEMU and got a LAB DF3 for 90 pounds... side by side, the only real difference we could both tell was my grip was white and his was black.

limiting_beliefs.belief: Only established putter brands (Scotty Cameron, TaylorMade, Ping, Odyssey) make legitimate putters
limiting_beliefs.how_it_limits: Creates a brand credibility barrier for Performance Golf entering the premium putter space. Golfers default to heritage brands because of tour validation, peer recognition, and resale value.
limiting_beliefs.counter_belief_needed: The biggest breakthroughs in putting (LAB's zero-torque, Evnroll's SweetFace) came from brands nobody heard of 5 years ago. Performance Golf's RS1 continues that pattern -- real engineering from a company not constrained by 'the way it has always been done.'

supporting_quotes.quote: Scotty is king. I had a fitting with all labs and none compared to my Newport 2
The PGA tour is where all the best technology is put into play by the best players in the world. ZT putters have been adopted by many pro's, but it's not widespread.
Similar to Rolex, they absolutely make a quality product.

limiting_beliefs.belief: Performance Golf is a digital training company, not a real equipment maker
limiting_beliefs.how_it_limits: Creates specific skepticism about RS1 quality, engineering depth, and manufacturing capability. Golfers may view it as a marketing company selling a rebranded OEM putter rather than a genuinely engineered product.
limiting_beliefs.counter_belief_needed: Performance Golf spent [X years] developing Roll Straight Technology with [named engineer/designer]. The RS1 is not a rebranded commodity -- it is purpose-built around a specific engineering insight: forward CG eliminates face drift.

supporting_quotes.quote: Buying from a non-traditional brand and regretting it
The guy who spent $500 on a Scotty because of the logo, not the engineering

limiting_beliefs.belief: Zero-torque / face-stable putters are just a fad
limiting_beliefs.how_it_limits: Dismisses the entire face-stability technology category as temporary hype, making golfers wait rather than invest. Creates social risk: 'I don't want to be the guy who fell for a fad.'
limiting_beliefs.counter_belief_needed: Face angle at impact accounting for 80%+ of putt direction is not a fad -- it is physics. The technology that addresses this will only grow. Early adopters of face-stable technology are already reporting 3-7 strokes saved per round. That is not a fad. That is an engineering advantage.

supporting_quotes.quote: I see these 'zero torque putters' as another fad. Golf fads reminds me of fashion fads in a lot of ways.
Seems like most people who really struggle with putting/yips/demons have moved to zero torque stuff. I don't see any advantage to it, just another fad
It will be a fad.
I gotta ask, are Zero torque putters just a flavor of the month?

limiting_beliefs.belief: Face-balanced / zero-torque putters sacrifice feel for stability
limiting_beliefs.how_it_limits: Creates the perception that face-stable putters are 'dead,' 'clinical,' or 'soulless' -- that you trade the sensory pleasure of putting for mechanical consistency. Feel-driven golfers reject this trade-off.
limiting_beliefs.counter_belief_needed: The RS1 does not sacrifice feel. Heavy Forward Weighting delivers face stability through CG location, not by deadening the putter head. You get the solid, responsive feel of a premium mallet with the face stability of forward-weighted engineering.

supporting_quotes.quote: I honestly dont like zero torque at all, I think for people who dont practice putting and play for fun sure its ok. But if you want to be a great putter you will sacrifice all feel and release
It's way too light and ironically enough I cant keep the face square. It also feels dead and looks like a nightmare.
My two biggest issues with the LABs I've tried are that I struggle with the shaft lean and don't like the feel off the aluminum face.
If LAB was to ever come out with their models in some other material than aluminum I would definitely give them a try again.

limiting_beliefs.belief: Toe hang is for skilled putters; face balance is for struggling putters
limiting_beliefs.how_it_limits: Frames face-stable putters as 'training wheels' -- an identity threat to golfers who pride themselves on skill. Suggests that choosing a face-stable putter is an admission of weakness.
limiting_beliefs.counter_belief_needed: Tour professionals use face-stable putters because they eliminate a mechanical variable, not because they lack skill. Adam Scott, Lucas Glover, and others chose face stability to maximize their elite strokes -- not to compensate for poor ones.

supporting_quotes.quote: for people who dont practice putting and play for fun sure its ok. But if you want to be a great putter you will sacrifice all feel and release which you need for great putting... toe hang is where its at
Notice the best putters in the world don't use lab, the guys that struggle use a lab
face balance is a crutch -- real putters learn to release the putter head

limiting_beliefs.belief: I have already tried everything and nothing works
limiting_beliefs.how_it_limits: Creates hopelessness and putting despair that paradoxically makes golfers LESS likely to try something new. The exhaustion of the putter carousel makes them skeptical of any new solution.
limiting_beliefs.counter_belief_needed: You have tried different putters, but they all share the same fundamental design flaw: rear CG that causes face drift. The RS1 is not another version of what you have already tried. It is the first putter where the CG sits in front of the shaft. You have never tried this.

supporting_quotes.quote: I, too, am 63 years old, and have had periodic bouts of the yips. Also, like you, I have tried a number of putting methods including long, belly, and short putters. Regular grip and cross-handed.
I have tried the forward press, counting 1,2,3, not grounding the club, bouncing the club, ball further forward in my stance, all of which has not helped my putting yips.
I've probably owned 15 putters in the last 10 years. Every time I think I've found the one.

limiting_beliefs.belief: $399+ is too much to spend on a putter
limiting_beliefs.how_it_limits: Price objection is the surface-level articulation of deeper fears: fear of wasted money, fear of no improvement, fear of looking foolish. The price itself is not the real objection -- the risk-adjusted value is.
limiting_beliefs.counter_belief_needed: At $399, the RS1 costs less than most premium drivers that you use 14 times per round. You will use this putter 30+ times per round. If it saves you 3 strokes per round over 50 rounds, that is 150 strokes for $399 -- $2.66 per stroke saved.

supporting_quotes.quote: $500+ is a lot of money to throw at a hope.
Lab putters are way too far above my pay grade.
I don't have $1000 for any club in my bag, but especially not when it is a hypothetical improvement.
It is by far the most I've have ever paid for a club, but I am glad I did.

limiting_beliefs.belief: In-store testing does not predict on-course performance
limiting_beliefs.how_it_limits: Even if a golfer tests RS1 and likes it, they may not trust their in-store experience. This creates a conversion gap between demo and purchase.
limiting_beliefs.counter_belief_needed: The RS1 is one of the few putters where the in-store experience DOES predict on-course results, because the face stability benefit is mechanical, not psychological. The face stays stable whether you are on the practice green or standing over a 4-footer for par.

supporting_quotes.quote: Everytime i demo them in store, i make everything. But i can also say the same with the other putters. Its so easy to putt in store. Its a different story on the course, and that is my fear.
Like sports teams, putters can look great on paper but perform poorly on grass!
Given how much these putters cost, I didn't want to make a purchase decision based only on how it performed in the studio.

limiting_beliefs.belief: My stroke type (arcing) is not compatible with face-stable putters
limiting_beliefs.how_it_limits: The belief that stroke arc determines putter type creates a self-filtering effect where golfers with arcing strokes dismiss face-stable putters entirely without testing them.
limiting_beliefs.counter_belief_needed: The RS1's Heavy Forward Weighting works WITH your natural stroke arc, not against it. Unlike zero-torque putters that fight an arcing stroke, the RS1's forward CG reduces unwanted face rotation while allowing your natural stroke path.

supporting_quotes.quote: I think my stroke has too much of a natural arc to it but if I try to change that it feels too manipulated
LAB putters aren't bad but zero torque isn't what everyone needs. Why would I want to change my consistent stroke to make a different putter work?
it all depends on how you stroke the putter. Some players have a strong arc, some have a slight arc, and some swing it straight back and through like a pendulum.

limiting_beliefs.belief: If this technology really worked, everyone on Tour would use it
limiting_beliefs.how_it_limits: Tour adoption is the ultimate credibility shortcut. Low tour adoption of face-stable putters is used as evidence that the technology does not provide meaningful advantage at the highest level.
limiting_beliefs.counter_belief_needed: Tour players have endorsement contracts, familiarity bias, and comfort with equipment they have used for decades. The question is not what tour players use -- it is what the engineering says. Face angle determines 80%+ of start line. Forward weighting keeps the face square. These are physics, not opinions.

supporting_quotes.quote: If ZT putters were that much better, more pros would use them, and more importantly, they would be winning tournaments. How many LAB putters have walked away with victory on the PGA tour this year? Zero?
Notice the best putters in the world don't use lab, the guys that struggle use a lab
My understanding is that L.A.B. doesn't pay anyone to use their putters, so these are professional golfers making their own choices.

belief_clusters.central_belief: My putting problems are my fault (technique), not equipment
related_beliefs: Practice is what makes you a good putter, not equipment
It's my stroke, not the putter
Buying equipment is 'buying your way out of trouble'
Face-balanced putters are training wheels for weak putters
belief_clusters.rs1_strategy: Do not attack this belief. Reframe it: 'Your stroke matters. The RS1 makes your stroke more effective by eliminating a variable you cannot control through technique alone.' Position RS1 as a skill amplifier, not a skill replacement.

belief_clusters.central_belief: Face angle at impact is the primary determinant of putt direction
related_beliefs: Face drift is the root cause of pushed and pulled putts
Conventional putters force you to steer the face square
The right technology eliminates face rotation as a variable
Zero-torque / face-stable technology genuinely works
belief_clusters.rs1_strategy: This is RS1's core message territory. Golfers who have had the face-angle epiphany are pre-qualified buyers. Marketing must educate those who have not yet had this epiphany by naming the problem ('face drift'), explaining the physics (CG behind shaft causes rotation), and showing the solution (forward CG eliminates it).

belief_clusters.central_belief: Confidence on the greens is both the cause and result of good putting
related_beliefs: Confidence is the most important factor in putting
Equipment that inspires trust breaks the negative spiral
Stress-free putting is the ultimate aspiration
Missing putts destroys confidence, which causes more misses
belief_clusters.rs1_strategy: Every marketing message must culminate in confidence. The engineering arc: face drift causes misses -> misses destroy confidence -> destroyed confidence causes tight grip -> tight grip causes more face drift -> RS1 breaks this cycle. Final payoff: 'stress-free,' 'trust,' 'calm over every putt.'

belief_clusters.central_belief: Premium putters must prove their value through measurable improvement
related_beliefs: $399+ is too much to spend on a putter
More expensive does not always mean better
The per-use cost argument justifies premium putter investment
Fear of wasting money is the real objection, not price itself
belief_clusters.rs1_strategy: Lead with the per-use argument (30+ strokes per round at $399 vs driver at $500+ for 14 swings). Follow with stroke-savings math. Address the real fear (wasted money) with a guarantee or trial period. At $399-429, RS1 is priced below LAB ($449-549) -- make this price advantage explicit.

belief_clusters.central_belief: I have tried everything and nothing consistently works
related_beliefs: The next putter might be 'the one'
Trying multiple putters and nothing feels consistently better
Grips, stances, lessons -- nothing sticks for more than a few rounds
The putter carousel never ends
belief_clusters.rs1_strategy: Acknowledge the exhaustion: 'We know you have tried everything.' Then differentiate: 'But every putter you have tried shares the same fundamental flaw: the CG sits behind the shaft, which causes face drift. The RS1 is the first putter where the CG sits forward. You have never tried this because it has never existed until now.'

belief_clusters.central_belief: Only established brands make real putters
related_beliefs: Tour player usage validates technology
Performance Golf is a training company, not an equipment maker
Heritage brands (Scotty, Ping) have earned trust through decades
New brands must prove themselves with data, not marketing
belief_clusters.rs1_strategy: RS1 must lead with engineering proof, not brand narrative. Chris McGinley as engineering authority. Independent test data (SAM PuttLab, Quintic). Direct comparisons to LAB, Scotty, Spider on face angle consistency. The story: 'We are not a putter brand trying to sell you on heritage. We are engineers who identified a problem (rear CG) and built the solution (forward CG).'

belief_clusters.central_belief: How a putter looks and feels matters as much as how it performs
related_beliefs: Premium putters should look good at address
Feel and sound at impact must be satisfying
Unconventional looks are a social risk
Visual confidence at address drives on-course confidence
belief_clusters.rs1_strategy: RS1's mallet design is a competitive advantage if it looks premium and conventional. Unlike LAB (which requires golfers to accept unusual aesthetics), RS1 can deliver face stability in a package that looks like a premium mallet. Marketing should feature at-address photography, close-up craftsmanship shots, and feel/sound descriptions.

core_beliefs: Confidence determines putting performance more than any single variable
Face angle at impact determines where the putt starts
Putting inconsistency is the primary score barrier for serious golfers
The right equipment can amplify skill (but cannot replace it)
My putting problems oscillate between being my fault and my equipment's fault
secondary_beliefs: Practice is essential but not sufficient without proper equipment
Peer results and data are more credible than brand marketing
A putter that ends the search carousel is worth significant investment
Premium putter pricing must be justified by measurable improvement
Face-stable technology represents a real engineering advancement
How a putter looks and feels must inspire confidence at address
peripheral_beliefs: Tour usage validates technology (strong for some, irrelevant for others)
Seasonal putter switching is necessary (minority belief)
Zero-torque is a fad (fading as data accumulates)
PE ownership destroys brands (specific to LAB context)
Cheap clones perform identically to premium originals (fringe but vocal)

must_believe.belief: Face drift is real and it is costing me strokes
must_believe.current_state: Most golfers experience face drift as 'pushed/pulled putts' or 'inconsistency' but do not understand the mechanical cause. They blame themselves, their stroke, or generic 'bad putting.'
must_believe.target_state: Golfer understands that face drift is a specific mechanical phenomenon caused by the CG sitting behind the shaft in conventional putters. This causes the face to rotate open/closed during the stroke, resulting in directional errors they cannot fully control through technique.
must_believe.bridge_needed: Educational content: 'Why Your Putts Miss Even When Your Stroke Feels Good.' Visual demonstrations showing CG location and face rotation. The word 'face drift' gives golfers a name for what they have always felt but could not articulate.

must_believe.belief: Forward weighting solves face drift in a way traditional putters cannot
must_believe.current_state: Golfers may be aware of face-balanced and zero-torque putters but do not understand forward CG weighting as a distinct approach. They group all 'face stable' putters together.
must_believe.target_state: Golfer understands that moving the CG forward (in front of the shaft) eliminates the mechanical cause of face drift. This is different from traditional face balance (which reduces but does not eliminate rotation) and different from lie angle balance (LAB's approach).
must_believe.bridge_needed: Simple visual: show CG location in a conventional putter vs RS1. The 'pencil test' or 'balance point' demonstration. Engineering proof that forward CG keeps the face stable through the entire stroke, not just at rest.

must_believe.belief: Performance Golf has the engineering credibility to build a premium putter
must_believe.current_state: Performance Golf is known as a digital training and content company. Golfers may see a putter from PG as a marketing play rather than genuine engineering.
must_believe.target_state: Golfer believes that PG invested in real engineering (Roll Straight Technology, Heavy Forward Weighting, Face Down Balance) developed by credible designers/engineers. The RS1 is not a rebadged OEM product.
must_believe.bridge_needed: Chris McGinley as engineering authority and source of truth. Manufacturing transparency. Independent testing data. Engineering video content showing the design process and technology rationale. Comparison data against LAB, Scotty, Spider.

must_believe.belief: $399 is a smart investment, not an expensive gamble
must_believe.current_state: Golfers see $399 as expensive for a putter, especially from a non-traditional brand. The fear of wasted money creates purchase paralysis.
must_believe.target_state: Golfer frames the purchase as cost-per-stroke-saved math: $399 / 150 strokes saved per season = $2.66 per stroke. Also: less expensive than LAB ($449-549), priced at parity with Scotty Cameron, less than most premium drivers.
must_believe.bridge_needed: Per-use rationalization content. Stroke-savings calculator. Price comparison to LAB and Scotty. Risk reduction: guarantee, trial period, or demo program. Testimonials with specific stroke-count data.

must_believe.belief: The RS1 is different from LAB Golf -- it is the next evolution, not a copy
must_believe.current_state: Informed golfers will immediately compare RS1 to LAB. If RS1 is perceived as a LAB imitation at a lower price, it lacks differentiation and becomes a commodity play.
must_believe.target_state: Golfer understands that RS1 uses a different engineering approach (forward CG weighting vs lie angle balance), solves the same problem (face stability) in a way that works with all stroke types, looks like a conventional premium mallet, and costs less.
must_believe.bridge_needed: Direct comparison content: 'RS1 vs LAB: Same Problem, Different Engineering.' Highlight RS1 advantages: works with arcing strokes (LAB vulnerability), conventional aesthetics (LAB vulnerability), no special grip required (LAB vulnerability), better feel/sound (LAB vulnerability), lower price (LAB vulnerability).

must_believe.belief: The RS1 will work with my natural putting stroke
must_believe.current_state: Many golfers believe face-stable putters require a straight-back-straight-through stroke. Those with arcing strokes self-exclude from the category.
must_believe.target_state: Golfer believes that RS1's forward weighting works WITH their natural stroke arc, reducing unwanted face rotation without requiring them to change their stroke mechanics.
must_believe.bridge_needed: SAM PuttLab data showing RS1 performance across multiple stroke types. Testimonials from golfers with arcing strokes. Clear messaging: 'The RS1 works with your stroke, not against it.'

must_believe.belief: This is the last putter I will need to buy
must_believe.current_state: Golfers on the putter carousel have heard this promise before and are skeptical. They need to believe this is genuinely different, not just another stop on the merry-go-round.
must_believe.target_state: Golfer believes that once they experience RS1's face stability, going back to a conventional putter will feel wrong (like LAB converts who describe traditional putters as feeling 'alive' in a bad way).
must_believe.bridge_needed: Long-term user testimonials. 'After 6 months, I tried my old putter and...' Lock-in narrative. The experience of face stability creates a permanent reference point that makes conventional putters feel inferior.

must_believe.belief: The RS1 looks and feels like a premium putter
must_believe.current_state: Golfers fear that engineering-forward putters look unconventional (LAB stigma) or feel 'dead' (zero-torque complaint).
must_believe.target_state: Golfer believes the RS1 looks like a tour-caliber premium mallet and feels solid, responsive, and confidence-inspiring at impact.
must_believe.bridge_needed: At-address photography from the golfer's POV. Premium packaging and unboxing experience. Sound/feel testimonials. Side-by-side aesthetic comparison with Scotty Phantom, TaylorMade Spider. 'The engineering is revolutionary. The putter looks like it belongs.'

must_not_believe.belief: The RS1 is just another gimmick from an internet marketing company
must_not_believe.how_to_address: Lead with engineering substance, not marketing flash. Chris McGinley as credibility anchor. Independent test data. Manufacturing transparency. No breathless superlatives -- let the engineering and results speak. Show the R&D process, not just the finished product.

must_not_believe.belief: This is basically a cheap LAB Golf knockoff
must_not_believe.how_to_address: Explicitly differentiate the engineering: forward CG weighting vs lie angle balance. Different physics, different feel, different look, different price point. RS1 does not need the special press grip. RS1 works with arcing strokes. RS1 is not 'LAB but cheaper' -- it is 'the next step beyond LAB.'

must_not_believe.belief: My stroke is too arced for this putter to work
must_not_believe.how_to_address: Provide SAM PuttLab or Quintic data showing RS1 performance across stroke arc types. Include testimonials from golfers who previously used toe-hang putters and switched successfully. Message: 'Forward weighting reduces face drift regardless of your stroke arc.'

must_not_believe.belief: I need to change my putting stroke to use this putter
must_not_believe.how_to_address: Contrast with LAB, where some golfers report needing to unlearn old habits. RS1 positioning: 'You do not need to change your stroke. The RS1 works with your existing mechanics. The only thing that changes is the face stays stable through your natural motion.' Set expectation of a 2-3 round adaptation period, not a stroke rebuild.

must_not_believe.belief: All putters perform the same and confidence is the only variable
must_not_believe.how_to_address: Present objective measurement data: face angle consistency, dispersion patterns, start line accuracy. Show that RS1 mechanically produces more consistent face angles at impact than conventional putters. The confidence boost is CAUSED by measurable performance improvement, not placebo.

affirmations: I know my putting is holding me back
The truth is I can't make short putts consistently
I believe face angle is everything in putting
The fact is I putt better on the practice green than the course
I know I need a putter that works with my stroke
The reality is I've tried everything and nothing sticks
I'm convinced my face is open at impact
I know practice is important but I also need the right tool
The best investment in my bag is the club I use the most
I finally feel confident over putts
negations: I can't make anything inside 5 feet
Nothing I try works for more than a few rounds
I can't buy a putt to save my life
I don't trust my putter under pressure
No putter is going to fix bad technique
I can't figure out what is wrong with my putting
I won't spend $400 on something that might not work
There is no magic wand in putting
I can't get comfortable with a face-balanced putter
I'm never going to be a good putter
qualifications: Maybe it is the putter, maybe it is me
I guess the putter may be the exception to the 'don't blame equipment' rule
It could be a different story on the course
I suppose if you are bad enough, anything might help
Perhaps my stroke has too much arc for face balance
It probably works for some people but not me
I think my putting is partly mental, partly mechanical
Maybe I just need to practice more
I wonder if a fitting would help
attributions: It's because my face opens through impact
The reason I miss is I get too handsy under pressure
My three-putts are caused by bad lag putting
The problem is I steer the putter face through the ball
I miss short putts because of inconsistent face rotation
The issue is my grip pressure goes up under pressure
My pushed putts are because the CG sits behind the shaft
The reason I lose confidence is the putter twists in my hands
My putting is bad because I've never been properly fit
The problem is my putter doesn't match my stroke type

rs1_specific_belief_gaps.description: Beliefs specific to the RS1 purchase decision that do not yet exist in the market and must be created through marketing

beliefs_to_create.target_belief: Forward CG weighting is a distinct engineering category from zero-torque and face balance
beliefs_to_create.why_it_matters: Without this belief, RS1 gets lumped into the 'just another face-stable putter' category and competes on price alone rather than engineering differentiation
beliefs_to_create.creation_strategy: Visual content showing the three categories side by side: traditional (CG behind shaft), face-balanced (CG under shaft), forward-weighted (CG in front of shaft). Name it: 'This is what we call Roll Straight Technology. It is not zero-torque. It is not face balance. It is forward weighting -- the next evolution in putter engineering.'

beliefs_to_create.target_belief: Face Down Balance (FDB) is a visible proof of the engineering
beliefs_to_create.why_it_matters: Golfers can test face balance at home (lay the shaft on your finger). RS1 needs its own simple proof test that golfers can demonstrate and share
beliefs_to_create.creation_strategy: Create a 'Revealer moment' equivalent for RS1 -- a simple physical demonstration that shows the engineering in action. The face-down resting position when placed on a flat surface. Make this a shareable, social-proof moment.

beliefs_to_create.target_belief: Performance Golf's entry into putters is a serious engineering commitment, not a brand extension cash grab
beliefs_to_create.why_it_matters: The biggest unique conversion barrier for RS1 is brand credibility in the hardware space. This belief must be established before any product claims are credible
beliefs_to_create.creation_strategy: Engineering origin story content: Why PG built a putter. The engineering team. The years of development. Chris McGinley's role. The specific insight (every putter has the CG behind the shaft -- what if we moved it forward?). Manufacturing process transparency.
